x: math for interaction design — roadmap the skill isn't "advanced math." it's a way of seeing : model the visual as a function, then use calculus / linear algebra to extract the geometric thing you need. this curriculum exists because of one caption on a wavy carousel: "calculating the derivative of that wave lets you rotate the cells to sit perfectly on the curve. as you pinch, we stretch the amplitude of a sine wave that the cells follow." that sentence secretly contains trigonometry the wave , a derivative tangent → rotation , and nearby curvature 2nd derivative and arc length an integral . become fluent in those moves and you can place and orient anything on anything . see the full decode in 08 worked example wavy carousel. the one mental move every interaction in this folder is an application of this. internalize it and specs write themselves. lessons 1. 01 modeling visuals as functions — the core mindset and the meta move 2. 02 trigonometry and the unit circle — sin/cos as circular motion; phase, frequency, amplitude 3. 03 derivative as tangent — calculus, read geometrically 4. 04 vectors and linear algebra — dot, cross, rotation matrices, normals 5. 05 parametric curves and splines — paths, béziers, tangents on any curve 6. 06 interpolation easing and springs — lerp, smoothstep, remap, spring as an ode 7. 07 oscillations noise and organic motion — damping, summed sines, no

01 — modeling visuals as functions the single habit that separates "i tweak values until it looks right" from "i can derive exactly what i need." the shift stop asking "what animation api do i call?" ask: what function describes this? and what does its derivative / normal / integral give me? once a visual is a function, the whole toolkit of calculus and linear algebra becomes a set of design controls. the meta move memorize this each one hands you a new geometric handle on the same object. that progression is the "level of thinking" you're chasing. worked micro example cells follow a wave . where is cell i ? → which way does it face so it sits flush on the curve? → tangent angle , and pinch stretches → because is proportional to , the tilt updates for free . one parameter, coherent result. full derivation: 08 worked example wavy carousel. why this is the high leverage skill it makes effects composable : change one parameter, everything downstream follows correctly. it makes them art directable : you expose the parameters of the function as sliders. it removes guesswork : no more "0.45 felt right" — you derive the relationship. try it in desmos: plot with sliders for . then plot its derivative underneath. drag and watch both change together. that coupling is the whole idea. related next: 02 trigonometry and the unit circle the geometric handles: 03 derivative as tangent, 04 vect

02 — trigonometry & the unit circle not "soh cah toa." the one idea that unlocks waves, rotation, and oscillation at once. the core insight and are the x and y of a point travelling around the unit circle. so a wave is circular motion projected onto a line. oscillation, rotation, and waves are the same phenomenon seen from different angles. once you see this, half of "creative math" collapses into one picture. anatomy of a wave amplitude — how tall. pinch to stretch changes this. frequency — how many wiggles. period = . phase — slides the wave sideways; also = "where on the circle it starts." things you can build immediately pulsing / breathing: driven by time . staggered wave across items: give item i a phase , so → a travelling wave through a list. circular layout: place item i at angle , position . bridge to rotation important a 2d rotation by angle is built entirely from sin/cos: so trig ↔ rotation ↔ linear algebra are one topic. continue in 04 vectors and linear algebra. try it desmos: plot parametrically for in → you get a circle. now plot → you get the wave. same , two viewpoints. related prev: 01 modeling visuals as functions · next: 03 derivative as tangent rotation math: 04 vectors and linear algebra summed/ damped waves: 07 oscillations noise and organic motion in shaders: 05 math for shaders hub: 🗺 roadmap

03 — calculus: the derivative as tangent you don't need symbol pushing. you need the geometric meaning of three operations. derivative = slope = tangent direction the derivative is the slope of the curve at . slope is a direction. that's why it gives you rotation. the six derivatives worth memorizing chain rule is why — the inner pops out. second derivative = curvature how hard it bends use it to squash or scale an element more on tight bends, or to drive "acceleration feel." integral = accumulation → arc length to space items evenly along a curve not evenly in , which bunches them on steep parts : in code you usually do this numerically walk the curve in tiny steps, sum segment lengths, then resample at equal distances . the progression to keep in your head try it desmos: plot a function and at a draggable point. drag it and watch the tangent line ride the curve — that line's angle is your rotation. related the payoff: 08 worked example wavy carousel tangents on arbitrary paths: 05 parametric curves and splines springs are differential equations: 06 interpolation easing and springs, damped harmonic oscillator prev: 02 trigonometry and the unit circle · hub: 🗺 roadmap

04 — vectors & linear algebra the native language of graphics. two products do most of the work. a vector is a direction with length position, velocity, normal, offset — all vectors. learn to think in vectors instead of separate / and the code shrinks. dot product = "how aligned are these two directions?" → same general direction · → perpendicular · → opposing. uses: lighting , "is the user dragging toward this?", projecting one vector onto another, finding angles. cross product 2d = "how perpendicular / which way does it turn?" in 2d it's a single signed scalar: sign = turn direction left/right, clockwise/ccw → winding, "which side of the line." magnitude = area of the parallelogram → signed area, overlap tests. perpendicular normal of is — rotating 90°. this is how you offset along a curve's normal. rotation is a matrix and it's just sin/cos translation + rotation + scale compose by multiplying matrices — this is exactly what and are. see 15 transform 3d. why this matters for the carousel the cell's rotation comes from a tangent vector ; its perpendicular offset uses the normal ; placing it on a circle uses a rotation matrix . same three tools, everywhere. try it take two draggable vectors in desmos or paper . compute and by hand as you move them; predict the sign before you calculate. build the reflex: dot = alignment, cross = turn. related rotation source: 02 trigonometry a

05 — parametric curves & splines generalize "the wave" to any path — circles, arcs, hand drawn strokes, béziers. parametric form: describe motion, not just shape instead of , use a parameter : this handles loops and verticals that can't, and the tangent falls right out of the derivative — same trick as 03 derivative as tangent, now on any path. examples bézier curves the workhorse a cubic bézier is a blend of 4 control points : = endpoints; = "handles" that pull the curve. its derivative also a bézier, one degree lower gives the tangent → orientation along the path. this is what , paths, and the cubic timing curve all are. see 16 cashapelayer. catmull rom curve through points béziers don't pass through their middle controls; catmull rom splines pass through every point — ideal for "lay cells along these positions smoothly." it's a few lines and converts to béziers for drawing. placing things along a curve 1. sample the curve → build a length table 03 derivative as tangent arc length . 2. resample at equal arc length for even spacing. 3. at each sample, take the tangent → rotate the element; take the normal → offset it. this is the wavy carousel recipe generalized to any path: 08 worked example wavy carousel. try it / swift charts / desmos: drop 4 control points, drag the handles, and render the tangent at . watch how the handles steer the orientation. related prereqs: 03 deriva

06 — interpolation, easing & springs the small toolkit you reach for in every falloff — ui or shader. deep companion: interpolation and easing. the five functions to know cold + are 80% of "make this value drive that effect smoothly." the stories strip edge falloff was exactly over a remapped distance. easing = reshaping before you lerp an easing curve is just a function applied to progress. ≈ smoothstep. cubic timing curves are béziers 05 parametric curves and splines . see 14 animation timing. springs = physics instead of a fixed curve a spring is a second order differential equation — which is why it connects back to calculus 03 derivative as tangent : critical damping → fastest settle with no overshoot . used in the perplexity dots' return. under damped → bouncy; over damped → sluggish. springs are retargetable mid flight no fixed duration → great for gestures. full treatment: damped harmonic oscillator and the wave library. when to use which need use map a position/scroll to an effect + a→b over a fixed time, designed feel easing curve / bézier timing gesture driven, interruptible, physical spring try it build a spring in a playground; expose and sliders. find critical damping by feel, then verify . related vault deep dives: interpolation and easing, damped harmonic oscillator, momentum and deceleration, rubber banding formula timing in ca: 14 animation timing, 11 caspring

07 — oscillations, noise & organic motion how to make motion feel alive instead of mechanical. damped oscillation = bounce that dies out the envelope is the secret to natural settling — it's the analytic cousin of the spring in 06 interpolation easing and springs. see damped harmonic oscillator. summed sines fourier intuition any periodic motion is a sum of sine waves. layer a few sines at different frequencies/amplitudes and you get rich, non trivial motion from trivial parts: use incommensurate frequencies not simple multiples so it never visibly repeats — that's the "gently alive, never looping" feel the perplexity dots' idle drift used exactly this . noise = controlled randomness with smoothness plain is jittery. perlin / simplex noise is smooth random — neighboring inputs give nearby outputs. that smoothness is what reads as organic smoke, flowing fields, hand tremor . sample noise over time for wandering motion: . sample over space for fields/turbulence: . domain warping feed noise into the input of more noise gives liquid, billowing motion. see 15 noise and patterns. recipe: "gently alive" element that layered structure is how the best ambient interactions are built. try it in a shader or playground, animate a dot with one sine, then add a second at an unrelated frequency, then add a touch of noise. feel each layer's contribution. related decay & springs: 06 interpolatio

08 — worked example: the wavy carousel the full decode of: "the derivative of that wave lets you rotate the cells to sit on the curve; as you pinch, we stretch the amplitude." everything in this folder, applied once. step 1 — model the curve as a function cells ride a sine wave along the horizontal axis : = amplitude the pinch target , = frequency, = phase. → 02 trigonometry and the unit circle step 2 — place each cell cell i sits at some its scroll position . its vertical offset: step 3 — derivative → tangent → rotation the slope at chain rule, 03 derivative as tangent : step 4 — pinch stretches amplitude and rotation follows for free map the pinch gesture's scale onto : because is proportional to , increasing amplitude automatically increases the tilt. one parameter drives shape and orientation coherently — that coupling is the craft. step 5 level up — even spacing via arc length equal spacing bunches cells on the steep parts. space them by arc length instead 03 derivative as tangent : sample the curve, build a length table, resample at equal . now cells are visually even no matter how tall the wave gets. step 6 extra polish — curvature scale/foreshorten cells slightly by curvature so they "hug" tight bends. → 03 derivative as tangent the whole thing in pseudo code build it yourself week 4–5 do it in swiftui first + per cell, driven by a 's scale , then understand it as a pur

x: 09 — what drives the value the ios bridge the math tells you what the value should be. this note tells you how ios should compute it over time. same three categories, every interaction. the three drivers driver the value is… ios tools example input driven a pure function or — no animation , , , gesture translation stories edge scale; the wavy carousel interpolated a tween a start→end the system blends swiftui , , ca implicit/explicit a button press, a sheet per frame imperative recomputed each frame , metal compute, manual loop perplexity dots; physics fields the carousel and the stories strip are category 1 — they are functions of gesture/scroll , not animations. that's why "no per frame effect, apply to the main composition" was the right instinct. how to choose 1. is the value a direct function of a gesture/scroll position? → pure function cleanest; prefer this . 2. is it "go from a to b with a nice feel"? → tween / spring . 3. does it need continuous simulation particles, fields, frame precise timing ? → per frame . why understanding core animation matters here swiftui and uikit are skins over core animation . the math you compute becomes layer transforms that the render server interpolates. knowing the substrate explains why category 2 tweens behave as they do model vs presentation layer and when to drop to category 3. substrate: core animation roadmap → 01 how rendering w

10 — study method the method matters more than the reading list. four habits. 1. desmos first, code second before writing any code, model the interaction as a function in desmos with sliders. take its derivative there; watch the tangent move. the interactivity builds intuition that reading can't. this is the most important habit on the page. 2. drill the meta move pick a curve wave, circle, spiral, bézier and extract its tangent orientation , normal perpendicular , and curvature bend . do it for five different curves until it's automatic. → 01 modeling visuals as functions, 03 derivative as tangent 3. reconstruct, math first you already reverse engineer interactions — keep doing it, but write the function and derive what you need before building. ask: what curve are the cells on? what's its derivative? what does the gesture change? write that spec, then code. this also trains the spec/pseudo code muscle — turn "scales as it enters" into with named constants and stated edge cases. 4. use shaders as the lab your metal work is applied math — , vectors, oscillation are already in there. when you learn a concept, immediately deploy it in a shader. theory + an existing playground = a fast feedback loop. → 05 math for shaders a good single rep 1. see an interaction you like. 2. guess the function + driver 09 what drives the value . 3. reproduce it in desmos with sliders. 4. derive the

t: 01 — Why Swift: History, Safety, and the Ecosystem
x: 01 — why swift: history, safety, and the ecosystem swift is the language apple created to replace objective c. understanding why it exists helps you understand why its features work the way they do. a brief history the key insight: swift was designed from scratch to fix objective c's pain points — messaging, manual memory management, c header files, pointer arithmetic — while keeping interoperability with existing apple frameworks. safety: swift's core philosophy swift's defining characteristic is safety by default . the compiler catches entire categories of bugs at compile time. optional safety — no more null pointer crashes this single feature eliminates the most common crash in software: the null pointer dereference. you'll explore optionals deeply in 08 optionals 08 optionals.md . type safety — no implicit conversions memory safety — arc does the work swift uses automatic reference counting arc to manage memory. no / , no garbage collector pauses. covered in depth in 18 memory management 18 memory management.md . performance swift is a compiled language. it's not interpreted like python or javascript — your code is compiled to native machine code. key performance features: value types — structs live on the stack, no heap allocation overhead copy on write — large collections only copy when modified whole module optimization — the compiler inlines and optimizes across files z

02 — variables and types every swift program starts with data. this lesson covers how to declare variables, what types exist, and how swift's type system keeps you safe. constant vs variable rule of thumb : use by default. switch to only when you need to mutate the value. xcode will warn you if you use but never mutate it. why this matters: immutability makes code easier to reason about. when you see , you know that value never changes. this becomes critical in concurrent code see 20 concurrency basics 20 concurrency basics.md . type inference vs explicit annotation swift can figure out types from context. you don't need to write them — but you can. convention : omit type annotations when the type is obvious from the right side. add them when it's ambiguous or when you want a different type than the default. basic types integer types use for almost everything. use sized types only when interfacing with specific apis or binary formats. floating point types use by default. use when an api requires it like some graphics apis . boolean string and character strings are covered in depth in 03 strings 03 strings.md . type conversion — no implicit conversion swift never converts types automatically. this prevents subtle bugs. the returning an optional is your first taste of optionals — see 08 optionals 08 optionals.md for the full story. tuples tuples group multiple values into a singl

03 — strings strings in swift are powerful, unicode correct, and safer than in most languages. the tradeoff is that string indexing works differently than you might expect. strings are value types a in swift is a struct, not a class. when you assign or pass a string, it copies though swift optimizes this with copy on write internally — see 19 value semantics 19 value semantics.md . string interpolation embed expressions inside strings with . this is the preferred way to build strings in swift. avoid string concatenation for building complex strings. interpolation is faster and more readable. multiline strings use triple quotes for strings that span multiple lines. special characters raw strings when you have lots of special characters, wrap the string in to disable escape sequences. unicode: swift strings are unicode correct every swift is a collection of values, where each is a unicode extended grapheme cluster — what a human perceives as a single character. this is why swift strings aren't integer indexable. character type string.index — not int! this is the most surprising part of swift strings for newcomers. you cannot index a string with an integer. substrings substring operations return a type, which shares storage with the original string for efficiency. convert to if you need to store it long term. common string operations string comparison building strings see learning

04 — collection types swift has three primary collection types: ordered , unordered, unique , and key value pairs . all three are generic, type safe, and value types. arrays an ordered collection of values. the most used collection in swift. creating arrays accessing elements modifying arrays iterating essential array methods , , and friends are covered in more depth in 23 functional programming 23 functional programming.md . closures like are explained in 07 closures 07 closures.md . array slices slicing returns an that shares storage with the original. indices are preserved, not reset to zero. sets an unordered collection of unique values. fast membership testing. creating sets membership and modification set operations dictionaries an unordered collection of key value pairs. keys must be . creating dictionaries accessing values modifying dictionaries iterating dictionaries dictionary methods choosing the right collection need use why ordered list of items preserves insertion order, index access unique values, fast lookup o 1 contains, automatic dedup key value mapping o 1 lookup by key ordered unique values + dedup sets don't preserve order performance characteristics if you're checking frequently, use a instead of an . common patterns grouping counting occurrences deduplication preserving order collection protocols , , are explored in 13 protocols 13 protocols.md . see lear

05 — control flow swift's control flow is familiar if you know other languages, but with important differences: is exhaustive and has no implicit fallthrough, encourages early returns, and runs cleanup code automatically. if / else if / else ternary operator if as an expression swift 5.9+ switch — the powerful one swift's is dramatically more powerful than in c/java. it must be exhaustive cover all cases and has no implicit fallthrough . basic switch compound cases ranges in switch tuples in switch value binding where clause for advanced pattern matching techniques matching enums with associated values, expression patterns, custom pattern matching , see 22 pattern matching 22 pattern matching.md . guard — early return is the opposite of : the code inside the block runs when the condition is false . the block must exit the scope , , , , . is the idiomatic way to unwrap optionals and validate preconditions. it keeps the "happy path" at the lowest indentation level. you'll use extensively once you learn optionals in 08 optionals 08 optionals.md . guard vs if for in loops iterating over collections ranges where clause in loops ignoring the loop variable while and repeat while labeled statements when you have nested loops, labels let you or a specific loop. defer schedules code to run when the current scope exits — no matter how it exits return, throw, break, etc. . multiple defers

06 — functions functions are the building blocks of swift programs. swift functions have a distinctive feature: argument labels that make call sites read like prose. basic syntax the anatomy: no return value single expression functions when the body is a single expression, is implicit. argument labels and parameter names swift functions can have two names for each parameter: an argument label used at the call site and a parameter name used inside the function . omitting argument labels use to omit the label. why argument labels? labels make code self documenting: default parameter values parameters with defaults can be omitted at the call site. place parameters with defaults at the end. variadic parameters accept zero or more values of a specific type. a function can have multiple variadic parameters swift 5.4+ , but the parameter after a variadic one must have a label. inout parameters — pass by reference by default, function parameters are constants . use to modify the caller's variable. is how swiftui's works under the hood. when you pass to a child view, you're essentially passing an inout reference. see learning swiftui/05 state and binding ../learning swiftui/05 state and binding.md for how uses this concept. functions as first class citizens functions can be assigned to variables, stored in collections, and passed as arguments. function types every function has a type, d

closures closures are self contained blocks of functionality that can be passed around and used in your code. they capture and store references to variables and constants from the surrounding context. functions are actually a special case of closures every function you have written so far is a closure with a name. full closure syntax the complete form of a closure includes parameter types, return type, and the keyword to separate the header from the body. a closure that takes two integers and returns their sum: shorthand syntax swift provides several ways to shorten closure expressions. understanding these progressions is essential because you will encounter all of them in real codebases. trailing closure syntax when a closure is the last argument, move it outside the parentheses: type inference the compiler knows the types from context: implicit return single expression closures can omit : shorthand argument names , , etc. replace named parameters: operator methods when an operator matches the closure signature: all six versions produce identical results. start with the full form when learning, then use shorter forms as they feel natural. capturing values closures capture variables from their enclosing scope by reference. the closure maintains a live connection to the variable, not a snapshot. the variable lives on even after returns because the closure captured it. capture li

optionals swift uses optionals to handle the absence of a value. unlike languages that use or implicitly, swift makes the possibility of "no value" explicit in the type system. this eliminates an entire class of bugs at compile time rather than at runtime. what is nil? in many languages, any reference can be , and accessing it causes a crash. swift takes a different approach: a regular variable can never be nil. if a value might be absent, you must declare it as an optional type. the compiler then forces you to handle the nil case before using the value. optional is an enum under the hood, is just a generic enum with two cases: writing is syntactic sugar for . you can even use the long form: understanding this enum structure makes every optional operation intuitive you are always just pattern matching on two cases. declaring optionals the distinction between and matters. the first is "maybe an array," the second is "definitely an array, but individual elements may be nil." force unwrapping the operator extracts the value from an optional. if the optional is nil, your program crashes. rule of thumb: avoid force unwrapping. it defeats the purpose of optionals. use it only when you can prove the value exists and a nil would indicate a programmer error. optional binding with if let the safe way to unwrap. the unwrapped value exists only inside the braces. shorthand syntax swift 5.7

enums enumerations in swift are far more powerful than in most languages. they are first class types that can have associated values, raw values, computed properties, methods, initializers, and protocol conformances. enums are value types, like structs. basic enum declaration an enum defines a type with a fixed set of related values. enums shine when you have a closed set of possibilities. the compiler knows all cases, which enables exhaustive statements. because the switch is exhaustive, no case is needed. if you add a new case to the enum later, the compiler will flag every switch that does not handle it. associated values each case can carry different types of data. this is what makes swift enums so expressive. associated values make enums perfect for modeling states, api responses, and domain events. raw values enums can have a raw type. each case gets a fixed value of that type. string raw values default to the case name: important: an enum can have either raw values or associated values, not both. caseiterable conform to to get an automatically generated collection of all cases. this is useful for building menus, pickers, and settings screens. note that only works automatically when no case has associated values. methods and computed properties enums can have methods and computed properties just like structs. mutating methods since enums are value types, methods that chan

structs vs classes swift gives you two primary ways to define custom types with properties and methods: structs and classes. the fundamental difference is that structs are value types copied on assignment and classes are reference types shared on assignment . this distinction affects everything from memory behavior to thread safety to api design. structs: value types when you assign a struct to a new variable or pass it to a function, swift copies the data. each variable holds its own independent copy. structs get an automatic memberwise initializer based on their stored properties. you do not need to write unless you want custom behavior. classes: reference types when you assign a class instance to a new variable, both variables point to the same object in memory. notice that even though is declared with , its properties can change because only prevents reassigning the reference, not mutating the object it points to. the identity test: === vs == checks if two values are equal same content . checks if two references point to the same object in memory. structs do not support because they do not have identity. they are just values. struct advantages thread safety since each copy is independent, structs can be used across threads without synchronization. predictability no shared mutable state means no spooky action at a distance. when you pass a struct to a function, you know it c

properties properties associate values with a type. swift has several kinds of properties, each serving a different purpose: stored properties hold data, computed properties calculate values on demand, and property observers react to changes. mastering properties is essential because they form the backbone of every type you write. stored properties the simplest kind. a stored property is a constant or variable that is part of an instance. if the struct instance itself is , none of its properties can be modified: for classes, on the instance only prevents reassigning the reference. properties marked can still change. lazy stored properties a is not calculated until it is first accessed. use this for expensive initialization that may not always be needed. rules for lazy properties: must be the first access mutates the struct to store the value cannot be not thread safe by default the instance must be for structs since accessing a lazy property mutates it computed properties a computed property does not store a value. it provides a getter and optionally a setter that calculates a value each time. get only computed property: get/set computed property: the keyword is the implicit name for the incoming value in a setter. you can also name it explicitly: . property observers property observers respond to changes in a stored property's value. they are called every time the value is set

methods methods are functions that belong to a type. they define the behaviors and actions that instances or the type itself can perform. swift supports instance methods, type methods, subscripts, and custom operators all of which let you build expressive, readable apis. instance methods an instance method is a function defined inside a type. it has access to all properties of that instance via . methods can take parameters and return values just like free functions. the difference is they operate on a specific instance. the self keyword refers to the current instance. it is usually implicit you can omit it when there is no ambiguity. use explicitly when a parameter name shadows a property common in initializers or for clarity in complex closures. mutating methods on value types structs and enums are value types. by default, their methods cannot modify properties. add to allow changes. a mutating method can even replace entirely: remember: you cannot call a mutating method on a constant. type methods type methods belong to the type, not to instances. use for structs and enums. for classes, cannot be overridden. use to allow subclasses to override. inside a type method, refers to the type itself, not an instance: subscripts subscripts provide shortcut access using bracket syntax. arrays and dictionaries use them, and you can define your own. read only subscripts can omit the key

protocols protocols define a blueprint of methods, properties, and other requirements that a type must fulfill. they are the foundation of swift's protocol oriented programming paradigm. where other languages lean on inheritance hierarchies, swift encourages composition through protocols. protocol declaration a protocol specifies what conforming types must provide. it declares required properties with or and methods without bodies . properties in a protocol use for read only or for read write. the conforming type can implement with a stored property, a computed property, or even a constant. methods in a protocol have no body conforming types supply the implementation. protocol conformance a type conforms to a protocol by implementing all its requirements. you can also add conformance in an extension, which is useful for separating concerns or conforming types you do not own: protocol inheritance protocols can inherit from other protocols, building up requirements. a type conforming to must satisfy all requirements from , , and . protocol composition combine multiple protocols into a single requirement using . this is more flexible than protocol inheritance because the combination is defined at the use site, not in the protocol hierarchy. you can also combine a protocol with a class type: protocol extensions: default implementations protocol extensions let you provide default be

generics generics let you write flexible, reusable functions and types that work with any type, while still being fully type safe at compile time. they are one of swift's most powerful features and the foundation of its standard library. see 13 protocols ../learning swift/13 protocols.md for protocol based constraints used throughout this file. why generics? without generics, you'd need to write separate functions for every type: this is repetitive and error prone. generics solve this with a single definition. generic functions a generic function uses a type parameter a placeholder name in angle brackets : is a type parameter . the compiler infers it from the arguments. you can have multiple type parameters: generic types you can define structs, classes, and enums that work with any type: the type parameter is determined when you create an instance. type constraints often you need the type parameter to have certain capabilities. use a type constraint : without , the operator would not be available. generic where clauses for more complex constraints, use a clause: the clause says: "c1 and c2 are both collections, and their element types must match." associated types in protocols protocols use instead of type parameters: you can constrain associated types: opaque types: the keyword hides the concrete return type while keeping the compiler fully aware of it: the caller sees . the

extensions extensions add new functionality to existing types even types you don't own, like , , or . they are swift's primary mechanism for organizing code, adding protocol conformance after the fact, and extending generic types with conditional capabilities. see 13 protocols ../learning swift/13 protocols.md for protocol conformance and 14 generics ../learning swift/14 generics.md for generic type extensions. adding computed properties extensions cannot add stored properties, but they can add computed properties: adding methods mutating methods work on value types: adding initializers extensions can add convenience initializers. for structs, you still get the default memberwise initializer: for classes, extensions can only add convenience initializers, not designated ones: adding protocol conformance one of the most powerful uses of extensions you can make any type conform to a protocol after the fact: conditional conformance in extensions you can make a generic type conform to a protocol only when its type parameter satisfies certain constraints: protocol extensions default implementations you can provide default method implementations for a protocol. all conforming types get them for free: extending generic types when extending a generic type, the type parameters are available in the extension body: you can also constrain extensions on generic types: @retroactive conformanc

t: 01 — SwiftUI Philosophy: Declarative UI and the View Protocol
x: 01 — swiftui philosophy: declarative ui and the view protocol swiftui is a declarative ui framework. instead of writing step by step instructions to build your interface, you describe what the interface should look like for a given state. swiftui handles the how. declarative vs imperative imperative uikit — describe how you create an object. you set properties. you add it to a parent. you configure constraints. if the data changes, you manually update the label again. declarative swiftui — describe what that's it. no creating objects, no adding to parents, no constraints. you describe the result, and swiftui figures out how to produce it. the view protocol every swiftui view conforms to the protocol: see 13 protocols ../learning swift/13 protocols.md for how protocols work in swift. the protocol requires exactly one thing: a computed property called that returns . you never call yourself — swiftui calls it when it needs to know what this view looks like. — opaque return types the keyword means "there is a specific concrete type here, but i'm not telling you what it is." see 14 generics ../learning swift/14 generics.md for opaque return types and . why not write ? because as your view grows, the concrete type becomes absurdly complex — something like . the opaque type hides this complexity while preserving type safety. views are structs, not classes see 10 structs vs classes ../

t: 02 — Views and Modifiers: Building Blocks of SwiftUI
x: 02 — views and modifiers: building blocks of swiftui every swiftui interface is built from views and modifiers. a view describes what appears on screen. a modifier transforms a view into a new view with different appearance or behavior. what is a modifier? a modifier is a method on that returns a new view wrapping the original. it does not mutate the existing view — it creates a new one. each modifier wraps the previous result. the final type is a nested chain of wrappers — but you never see this because hides it. modifier order matters this is the single most important thing about modifiers. the order changes the result because each modifier wraps the view that came before it. think of it like wrapping a gift: padding first makes the box bigger, then background paints the bigger box. background first paints the small box, then padding adds clear space around it. visual proof the border modifier is your best debugging tool — it shows exactly where each layer of the view ends. common modifiers spacing and sizing colors and styles typography shape and clipping effects overlay and background with alignment places content on top of a view. places content behind it. both respect alignment. conditional modifiers ternary in modifier values preferred avoid for modifiers when possible using in creates different view types for each branch, which breaks animations and view identity: custo

01 — swiftui is a dependency graph the single most important mental model: swiftui is not a view tree that you redraw. it's a graph of values and the views that depend on them. when a value changes, swiftui re evaluates only the views that read it . everything in this series is a consequence of that one idea. the graph attributegraph underneath swiftui is a private framework called attributegraph . think of it as a spreadsheet: each attribute is a cell: a piece of state, a derived value, or a view's . edges are "depends on" relationships: depends on the it reads. when a cell changes, the graph marks everything downstream invalid dirty . on the next run loop, swiftui re evaluates only the dirty attributes — it recomputes those s, diffs the result, and updates the rendered layers. you never tell swiftui "re render." you mutate a value; the graph figures out who cared. "reading a value" is what creates the edge the dependency edge is created the moment your reads the value during evaluation. this is the crux: if you don't read it, you don't depend on it, and you won't be invalidated by it. this is why is efficient lesson 05 and why "my view isn't updating" almost always means "the edge you assumed exists was never created" lesson 12 . three things swiftui needs for each view from wwdc's demystify swiftui , every view has: 1. identity — which view is this, across updates? lesson 02

02 — view identity and lifetime state persists because of identity . if you don't understand identity, you'll hit the two classic bugs: state that resets unexpectedly , and state that refuses to reset when you want it to . identity ≠ the view struct the view struct is recreated constantly lesson 01 . so swiftui can't use "is this the same struct instance?" to decide whether state carries over. instead it uses identity : a stable notion of "this is the same ui element as last update." state , , the storage behind owned by is keyed to identity : same identity across updates → same storage → state persists. new/changed identity → fresh storage → state is reset and old state is destroyed . two kinds of identity 1. structural identity the default swiftui infers identity from a view's position in the view tree — the shape of your . as long as the structure is stable, the view at a given position keeps its identity. the creates two distinct identities . flipping destroys one and creates the other — their state does not transfer. ⚠️ erases structural identity. swiftui can't see the shape through it, so it often can't preserve identity across updates → state loss + worse diffing. avoid in hot paths lesson 11 . 2. explicit identity you assign identity yourself: — each row's identity is its . — forces an identity; changing the value destroys and recreates the view and resets its state . t

11 — invalidation and performance now that you know how updates propagate, you can control how often . performance work in swiftui is almost entirely about not creating dependencies you don't need and not destroying identity you wanted to keep . the cost model re evaluation is cheap ish but not free — it allocates value type view trees and diffs them. the expensive failures are: a a huge subtree re evaluating on every keystroke, b work done inside that should be cached, c lost identity forcing teardown/rebuild lesson 02 , d defeating the diffing. goal: each state change re evaluates the smallest set of bodies, and each does the least work. lever 1 — read narrowly fewer edges the edge is the read lesson 01/05 . so: read only what you display. don't read in a parent if only a leaf needs one field — pass the object down and let the leaf read its field. split big views. a monolithic that reads 10 properties re runs on any of the 10. extract subviews so each reads only its slice. this is the highest leverage fix for "whole screen re renders." for model fields that never drive ui caches, tokens so reads don't subscribe lesson 05 . lever 2 — preserve identity avoid teardown avoid in hot paths — it erases structural identity and tanks diffing lesson 02 . prefer , , or generics. stable ids — never the array index when the array can reorder/insert; use a stable model id. wrong ids cause

core animation curriculum roadmap this curriculum takes you from zero to mastery of core animation — the rendering engine that powers every pixel on ios, macos, tvos, and watchos. whether you see a uikit view animate across the screen or a swiftui transition dissolve, core animation is doing the heavy lifting underneath. why core animation? uikit's and swiftui's are convenience wrappers. they ultimately create objects on trees. understanding core animation gives you: precise control over every animation parameter the ability to build effects that high level apis cannot express deep performance insight — you'll know why frames drop the vocabulary to read and write render level code in any apple framework see 12 animation basics.md ../learning swiftui/12 animation basics.md — swiftui animations ultimately compile down to core animation. prerequisites required: complete learning swift lessons 01 through 13 types, control flow, functions, closures, structs, classes, protocols, enums, optionals, error handling, extensions, generics, and access control . helpful: basic uikit familiarity uiviewcontroller, uiview, storyboards or programmatic layout . you do not need to be an expert. not required: swiftui knowledge. you can study both curricula in parallel. see 00 roadmap.md ../learning swift/00 roadmap.md for the swift prerequisite path. curriculum structure — 6 parts, 25 lessons part

how rendering works before writing a single animation, you need to understand what happens between your code changing a property and photons hitting the user's retina. this lesson maps the entire ios rendering pipeline. the rendering pipeline every frame on ios passes through this pipeline: your app does not render pixels. it builds a description the layer tree and hands it off. the render server — a separate system process — does the actual gpu work. this is why core animation is fast: your app can proceed to the next frame while the render server is still compositing the previous one. the four phases in your app each frame, core animation runs these phases inside your app process: 1. layout / are called autolayout constraints are solved frames and bounds are calculated 2. display / are called if is set core graphics drawing commands execute into a backing store bitmap this is the most expensive phase if you do custom drawing 3. prepare image decoding compressed png/jpeg → raw bitmap image format conversion if needed e.g., non gpu friendly formats 4. commit the layer tree snapshot is serialized sent to the render server via ipc mach ports everything is batched — nothing renders mid run loop the three layer trees core animation maintains three parallel copies of every layer: model layer the layer object your code touches: reflects the target state — where things should end up a

implicit animations core animation was designed around a radical idea: changing a property should animate automatically. when you set a layer's to , it doesn't snap it fades. this behavior is called an implicit animation, and it is the default for every standalone . see 02 calayer fundamentals.md ../learning coreanimation/02 calayer fundamentals.md this lesson builds on the layer properties introduced there. how implicit animations work when you change an animatable property on a , core animation intercepts the change and creates a behind the scenes. the layer transitions from the old value to the new value over a default duration of 0.25 seconds. tap the screen and the layer smoothly transitions from blue to red over 0.25 seconds. you wrote zero animation code. animatable properties not every property animates implicitly. the following properties do: property type what animates cgpoint layer moves cgrect layer resizes float layer fades cgcolor color transitions cgfloat corners round/square catransform3d scale, rotate, translate cgfloat border grows/shrinks cgcolor border color transitions cgfloat shadow blur changes cgsize shadow moves float shadow fades cgcolor shadow color transitions cgpath shadow shape morphs any cgimage cross fades image cgrect content crop animates catransform3d child perspective changes if a property is not in this list such as , , or setting it takes e

catransaction every property change you make on a is part of a transaction. is the mechanism core animation uses to batch property changes into atomic commits, ensuring that related changes are rendered together in the same frame. without transactions, each property change would render independently, causing visual tearing and inconsistency. see 07 implicit animations.md ../learning coreanimation/07 implicit animations.md implicit animations rely on transactions to control their timing and behavior. implicit transactions you do not need to create a transaction manually. every run loop cycle, core animation wraps all property changes in an implicit transaction. when the run loop finishes, the transaction commits and all changes render together. all three animations begin simultaneously when the current run loop cycle ends, because they are batched into one implicit transaction. explicit transactions wrap property changes in and to create an explicit transaction with custom parameters. all three properties now animate over 1 second instead of the default 0.25. setting animation duration controls how long implicit animations last within the transaction. this only affects implicit animations. explicit animations those you create with use their own property. setting the timing function controls the easing curve for all implicit animations in the transaction. available built in timin

caspringanimation simulates a physical spring attached to a layer property. instead of defining an animation curve mathematically, you define physical properties mass, stiffness, damping, and initial velocity and the simulation produces natural, organic motion. this is the same physics engine that powers uikit's spring animations and swiftui's modifier. see 09 cabasicanimation.md ../learning coreanimation/09 cabasicanimation.md caspringanimation is a subclass of cabasicanimation, so everything from that lesson applies here. creating a spring animation the four parameters mass default: 1.0 the weight of the object on the spring. heavier objects take longer to accelerate and decelerate, producing slower, more lumbering motion with more momentum. stiffness default: 100.0 the force of the spring. higher stiffness means the spring pulls harder toward the target, producing snappier motion. damping default: 10.0 friction that resists the spring's motion. higher damping means less oscillation. this is not the same as swiftui's more on that below . initialvelocity default: 0.0 the starting speed of the animation in points per second. positive values move toward the target, negative values move away. this is critical for gesture driven animations where you want the animation to match the user's finger velocity. settlingduration is a computed property that tells you how long the spring si

caanimationgroup individual animations target a single property. lets you synchronize multiple animations position, scale, opacity, color so they play together as a coordinated unit with shared timing, a single completion callback, and the ability to stagger start times. any subclass can be included: , , , or even nested groups. see 09 cabasicanimation.md ../learning coreanimation/09 cabasicanimation.md , 10 cakeyframeanimation.md ../learning coreanimation/10 cakeyframeanimation.md , and 11 caspringanimation.md ../learning coreanimation/11 caspringanimation.md groups combine these individual animation types. creating an animation group all three animations start simultaneously and run over 0.6 seconds. group timing vs individual timing the group's is the master clock. sub animation durations interact with it in important ways: if a sub animation has no duration set 0.0, the default , it inherits the group's duration. if a sub animation has a shorter duration than the group, it finishes early and holds its final value for the remainder. if a sub animation has a longer duration than the group, it gets clipped at the group's duration. staggered animations with begintime each sub animation has a property. within a group, this is relative to the group's start not to . the stagger creates a cascading effect: first the layer fades in, then it scales, then it moves. repeating and autor

catransition and animate property changes a layer moves, fades, or scales. is a different paradigm: it animates content changes. when you swap an image, change text, or replace a sublayer, a transition provides the visual bridge between old content and new content. think page curls, pushes, fades, and reveals. see 09 cabasicanimation.md ../learning coreanimation/09 cabasicanimation.md catransition inherits from caanimation but operates on layer content rather than individual properties. creating a transition the key step: add the transition to the layer, then immediately change the content. core animation captures the old content as a snapshot and animates from the snapshot to the new content. transition types defines the animation style: type effect cross dissolve between old and new content new content slides in over the old content new content pushes old content out old content slides away to reveal new content underneath subtypes specifies the direction for directional transitions , , : subtype direction content enters from the left content enters from the right content enters from the top content enters from the bottom ignores the subtype since it has no direction. private transition types core animation includes several private transition types that are not part of the public api. they work but are undocumented and may be rejected by app store review if detected. use them

animation timing animation timing is the difference between an animation that feels mechanical and one that feels alive. this lesson covers for pacing curves and the protocol for controlling playback — start time, speed, repetition, fill modes, and the powerful technique of scrubbing animations by hand. camediatimingfunction a defines the pacing curve of an animation — how the interpolated value progresses over time. internally, it is a cubic bezier curve with two control points. built in timing functions see 09 cabasicanimation.md ../learning coreanimation/09 cabasicanimation.md for the full cabasicanimation api. custom cubic bezier when built in curves are not enough, create your own with control points. the curve goes from 0, 0 to 1, 1 . you supply the two intermediate control points: visualizing the bezier curve the control point graph has time 0 to 1 on the x axis and progress 0 to 1 on the y axis. the curve determines how far along the animation is at any given moment: a steeper slope means faster motion at that point. a flat slope means the animation is nearly paused. the camediatiming protocol both and adopt . this protocol provides fine grained playback control. begintime delays when the animation starts. measured in the parent's time space usually the layer's time, which maps to . duration how long the animation takes, in seconds. default is 0, which inherits the tran

3d transforms is a 4x4 matrix that positions layers in three dimensional space. while the screen is flat, core animation can simulate perspective projection, letting you build card flips, rotating carousels, and cube transitions entirely on the gpu. catransform3d basics a is a struct with 16 fields through representing a 4x4 homogeneous transformation matrix. you rarely touch the fields directly — apple provides constructor and combination functions. identity the identity transform means "no transformation applied": translation move a layer along x, y, and/or z axes: moving along z pushes the layer "into" or "out of" the screen. without perspective, this has no visible effect orthographic projection . we'll fix that shortly. scale rotation rotation takes an angle in radians and an axis vector x, y, z : concatenating transforms combine transforms by multiplying them. order matters — matrix multiplication is not commutative. the versions create a fresh transform. the non versions , , apply to an existing transform, effectively concatenating. the m34 perspective trick by default, core animation uses orthographic projection — no foreshortening. a layer rotated around y just gets narrower, like squishing it, rather than showing depth. to enable perspective, modify of the transform matrix. this value controls the vanishing point distance: m34 value effect subtle, far perspective stan

careplicatorlayer efficiently clones its sublayers with incremental transforms, color shifts, and animation delays. you provide one sublayer, and the replicator draws n copies — each offset, rotated, or tinted from the previous. this is vastly more efficient than creating n separate layers, and the staggered animation delay produces elegant ripple and cascade effects. core properties the replicator does not create new objects in memory. it reuses the same render data with different transforms applied by the gpu. this is why 100 copies cost almost the same as 1. see 06 sublayer management.md ../learning coreanimation/06 sublayer management.md for the standard sublayer management api that careplicatorlayer replaces in high copy count scenarios. instancetransform the is applied cumulatively to each copy. copy 0 the original gets no transform. copy 1 gets one application. copy 2 gets two applications. and so on. color offsets each copy can shift its color channels incrementally: the offsets are additive per copy. copy 3's red = base red + 3 redoffset . values are clamped to 0, 1 . dot loading indicator three dots that bounce with staggered timing: the means dot 1 starts its animation 0.15s after dot 0, and dot 2 starts 0.3s after dot 0. this creates the cascading pulse effect. activity spinner dots arranged in a circle with fading opacity — the classic loading indicator: each dot f

catiledlayer is designed for one specific problem: displaying content that is far too large to fit in memory as a single bitmap. maps, pdf documents, gigapixel images anything where the full resolution would consume hundreds of megabytes of ram. catiledlayer solves this by breaking the content into tiles and drawing each one on demand, on a background thread. see 04 layer contents.md ../learning coreanimation/04 layer contents.md catiledlayer replaces the standard approach of setting to a single cgimage with a dynamic tiling system. how tiling works when a catiledlayer needs to display, it: 1. divides its bounds into a grid of tiles default 256x256 points . 2. determines which tiles are currently visible in the viewport. 3. calls on a background thread for each visible tile. 4. the context's clip rect tells you exactly which tile region to draw. 5. newly drawn tiles fade in over seconds. 6. off screen tiles are cached but can be purged under memory pressure. this means the layer never holds the entire content in memory. only the visible tiles plus a small buffer exist at any time. basic setup the critical insight: receives the rect for one tile, not the entire layer. you only draw content for that tile region. key properties tilesize the size of each tile in points. larger tiles mean fewer draw calls but more memory per tile. smaller tiles mean faster initial display but more o

x: 01 — gpu vs cpu: why shaders exist the core problem your iphone screen has 2.7 million pixels 1179 x 2556 on iphone 15 pro . at 120fps, that's 324 million pixel calculations per second . a cpu processes things one at a time or a few at a time with multiple cores . a gpu processes thousands at a time . cpu: the smart worker the cpu is great at complex, sequential logic — if/else chains, branching, state machines. but it's terrible at doing the same simple operation millions of times. gpu: the army of simple workers the gpu is terrible at complex branching but incredible at doing the same operation on different data simultaneously. this is called simd single instruction, multiple data . what is a shader? a shader is a small program that runs on the gpu . it's called a "shader" because originally these programs were used to calculate lighting and shading in 3d graphics. today they do much more. think of it like this: three types of shaders 1. fragment shader pixel shader runs once per pixel . determines the final color of each pixel on screen. this is what you'll use most for ios visual effects. 2. vertex shader runs once per vertex corner point of a triangle . transforms 3d geometry. 3. compute shader kernel general purpose gpu computation. not tied to rendering. metal shading language msl apple's shader language is called metal shading language msl . it's based on c++14 with gpu

t: Metaball — Blur + Color Matrix Threshold Trick
x: metaball — blur + color matrix threshold trick two draggable blobs that merge organically using zero shader code — just gaussian blur + alpha channel threshold via color matrix. the simplest way to get liquid/metaball effects. how it works the trick the entire effect is just two compositing operations: when two blurred circles overlap, their alpha values add up. the color matrix then thresholds the alpha — anything above a certain value becomes opaque, everything below becomes transparent. this creates the organic "merging blob" shape. the color matrix the alpha row is the key : input alpha output alpha result 0.0 30 → 0 fully transparent 0.3 12 → 0 still transparent 0.5 0 → 0 threshold edge 0.51 0.6 → clamped starts appearing 0.6 6 → 1 fully opaque 1.0 30 → 1 fully opaque the steep ramp multiply by 60 creates a sharp cutoff around alpha 0.5. where two blurred circles overlap, their alphas sum past this threshold, creating the smooth merge. geometry + gesture color sweep gradient from the origin creates a cyan→blue→cyan color band: compositing stack the with a prop means: render children into an offscreen buffer, apply the paint effects blur + color matrix , then composite back. metal shading language translation approach a: cifilter chain simplest — no metal code the most direct translation uses core image, which is what this technique maps to: approach b: swiftui canvas + blu

blur circles — noise driven organic motion two circles moving on simplex noise paths — one sharp with a sweep gradient, one blurred — clipped against each other to create a lens/distortion illusion. how it works noise driven position each circle's position is driven by 2d simplex noise, not random: why simplex noise : random motion is jittery. sine waves are repetitive. simplex noise creates smooth, organic, non repeating paths — like floating in water. two different noise generators called twice produce independent, uncorrelated paths. compositing stack the illusion : where circle 2 overlaps circle 1, you see a blurred version of circle 1's gradient. outside the overlap, circle 1 is sharp. this creates a lens/magnifying glass effect. animation loop the clock ping pongs between 0 and 20000, which feeds into the noise function. since noise is continuous, the motion smoothly reverses. metal shading language translation approach a: swiftui canvas + simplex noise approach b: metal fragment shader render the entire effect in a single fragment pass: gameplaykit noise built in for production, use apple's built in noise: key takeaway simplex noise + clip masking = organic lens effects. the same technique works for: floating particles ambient background animations liquid/water surface simulation organic ui transitions related 05 fractal glass — similar clip + blur, but with grid mask 04

13 — blur effects blur is the most important visual effect in ios — used in navigation bars, sheets, control center, notifications, and every glass material. this lesson builds from the simplest blur to apple quality implementations. why blur is hard a naive blur reads many neighboring pixels for every output pixel. for a 20px radius, that's texture reads per pixel. at 2.7 million pixels, that's 4.5 billion reads per frame . we need smart algorithms to make this fast. level 1: box blur simplest average all pixels in a square region. simple but produces boxy artifacts. cost : o radius^2 per pixel. unusable for large radii. level 2: separable blur 2 pass key insight: a 2d gaussian can be decomposed into two 1d passes horizontal then vertical . this reduces o r^2 to o 2r . swift side — two render passes: level 3: optimized gaussian linear sampling trick by sampling between two pixels , you get the interpolated value of both — effectively 2 samples for the price of 1. this halves the number of texture reads. 5 texture reads instead of 9 for the same quality. at larger radii, the savings compound. level 4: kawase blur best quality/performance ratio used by many game engines and real time renderers. instead of one large radius blur, apply multiple small blurs at increasing sample distances. each pass is just 5 taps. swift : run 4 6 passes, ping ponging between two textures: 5 texture

t: 03 — Q1: What Are You Changing? (The *What* Axis)
x: 03 — q1: what are you changing? the what axis the single most decisive question. it determines whether you stay in the cheap, high rungs or must descend to pixels. get this wrong and you either over engineer writing a shader to move a box or hit a wall trying to ripple pixels with . the five categories every animatable thing falls into one of these. identify yours first. 1. transform & opacity — the free tier position, scale, rotation, anchor, alpha, with a shaped layer , shadow with . these are composited by the gpu without redrawing content. the render server already has the layer's rasterized contents; it just multiplies a matrix and an alpha. animating these is nearly free even for hundreds of layers. stay at the highest rung. swiftui , or uikit / , or . rule: if your effect can be expressed as "the same pixels, moved/scaled/faded", you never need to go lower than swiftui. see 06 cost model cpu gpu for why these are free and what silently isn't e.g. animating on a non shaped layer triggers redraws . 2. per pixel procedural change — the shader tier color shifts that vary per pixel, warping/distortion, ripples, dissolves, mask reveals driven by noise, glow/bloom, chromatic aberration, animated gradients with non linear math, blur that you control. the defining trait: the output of each pixel is computed, not just repositioned. no transform can express it. this is the only cat

t: 04 — Q2: What Drives the Change Over Time? (The *Timing* Axis)
x: 04 — q2: what drives the change over time? the timing axis once q1 tells you what tier you're in, q2 picks the clock — who advances the animation each frame. this is where most "it stutters / it can't be interrupted / it feels robotic" problems are actually decided. the two clocks recap from 01 the rendering stack map 1. system interpolation clock — you give a, b, and a curve; the system ticks every frame for you. you never see a frame. 2. manual clock cadisplaylink — you get a callback per refresh and compute the value yourself. everything below is a flavor of one of these. the five timing drivers a. declarative interpolation — system clock "go from a to b over 0.3s with ease in out." the default. the system owns the frames. swiftui / uikit core animation use when: the motion is fully known up front and nothing external influences it mid flight. tradeoff: you can't easily redirect it once started without dropping to interruptible tools — 05 how the user touches it . b. scripted sequence — system clock a multi step canned animation: scale up → overshoot → settle; or a staged reveal of several elements. swiftui loop through discrete phases / independent tracks per property . uikit/ca + , with offsets for staggering. → learning coreanimation/12 caanimationgroup use when: the choreography is fixed but multi step. this is the rung "fine layer timing" lives on when you need exact pe

t: 05 — Q3: How Does the User Touch It? (The *Interaction* Axis)
x: 05 — q3: how does the user touch it? the interaction axis two interactions can change the same thing q1 with the same driver q2 and still need different stacks — because of how the user touches them . this axis is the tiebreaker, and it's the one most often discovered too late. the three interaction models 1. fire and forget tap a button → it animates → done. the user cannot grab it mid flight. the animation runs to completion uninterrupted. any high rung works. swiftui , , . simplest case — no special machinery. examples: a checkmark drawing in, a toast sliding up, a screen push. 2. interruptible / redirectable the user can grab the thing while it's animating and send it somewhere else — and it must respond smoothly, carrying its current velocity, not snapping or restarting. this is the rung that plain declarative animation cannot do well. a in flight, if you start a new one, jumps. you need one of: — pause, reverse, retarget; designed for this. additive core animation — multiple animations compose instead of replacing, so a new one blends from the current presentation state. → apple reverse engineered/core animation smooth 60 120fps spring physics with velocity transfer wave — a new spring starts from the current value and current velocity , so redirection is seamless. → maths physics/damped harmonic oscillator examples: a sheet you can grab mid dismiss, a card mid fling, pip

t: Understand and Eliminate Hangs from Your App
x: understand and eliminate hangs from your app wwdc21 session 10258 presented by anubhav , os performance team at apple summary this session teaches how to identify, diagnose, and eliminate hangs periods of unresponsiveness where an app appears stuck, lags, or spins. it covers the fundamental architecture of the main thread and the run loop, explains why hangs happen, demonstrates diagnostic tools like time profiler and system trace, introduces metrickit for field diagnostics, and walks through common anti patterns and their fixes using a demo app called desserted a recipe app for drinks and desserts . what is a hang? a hang is when an app becomes unresponsive to user input. the user taps, swipes, or otherwise interacts, but nothing happens the ui freezes, animations stall, and the app feels broken. formal definition a hang is reported when the main thread is busy doing work or waiting on another thread or system resource , causing a delay in updating the view content by at least 250 milliseconds . why 250ms? research shows users perceive ui updates within 100ms as "instant" a delay of 250ms or more is definitively noticeable and frustrating events are buffered during a hang and cannot be handled, compounding the problem if the hang is long enough, the system watchdog will terminate the app entirely hang severity categories category duration user impact micro hang < 400ms brief s

uikit internals — overview swiftui is the future, but it is a thin declarative skin over machinery that is still pure uikit and core animation underneath. every , every navigation push, every gesture you write in swiftui ultimately becomes a , a transition, or a . when an interaction misbehaves — a scroll that fights your gesture, a cell that recycles wrong, a hit test that misses — the answer is almost always one layer down, in uikit. this course is the substrate. it explains how uikit actually works so that the fluid interactions you build on top behave predictably. the mental model uikit is built from three intertwined systems: the view hierarchy decides what is drawn and where. the responder chain decides who handles events. the run loop decides when any of it happens. master those three and almost every uikit mystery dissolves. what you'll learn 1. 01 view controller lifecycle — the exact order of → → layout → , and why putting work in the wrong one causes bugs. 2. 02 responder chain — how touches and actions travel from a view up through controllers, and how and really resolve. 3. 03 uiscrollview mechanics — , , paging, deceleration, and the delegate callbacks that drive scroll linked effects. 4. 04 compositional layout — : items, groups, sections, and fractional sizing. 5. 05 diffable data sources — snapshot based updates that animate correctly and end an entire class of

view controller lifecycle a is a coordinator between a chunk of screen its and the rest of your app. its lifecycle methods fire in a precise order, and the single most common uikit bug is doing the right work in the wrong method. the order of events the two distinctions that matter most: runs once; runs every time the view appears. set up subviews and one time configuration in . refresh data that may have changed e.g. after returning from a pushed screen in . frames are not valid until . in , is often still the size from the nib/storyboard, not the real device size. never compute positions from in . why "don't read frames in viewdidload" matters can fire many times rotation, keyboard, trait changes , so keep it cheap and idempotent — assign frames, don't allocate. appearance is not loading a controller's can be loaded in memory without being visible . tells you the first; lets you touch the view without forcing it to load. accessing triggers immediately — a subtle cause of "why did my view load so early?" containment custom container controllers carousels, custom tab bars must keep the parent/child relationship correct or child lifecycle methods won't fire: skipping these calls is why a child's or rotation callbacks silently never run. related the view's geometry: layer geometry next: 02 responder chain · hub: 00 overview

the responder chain every interactive object in uikit — views, view controllers, the app itself — is a . events touches, presses, motion, remote control travel along a chain of responders until something handles them. understanding the chain explains hit testing, , with , and the keyboard. two phases: find, then bubble phase 1 — hit testing top down . when a touch lands, uikit walks the view hierarchy from the window downward to find the deepest view that contains the point and is eligible: a view is skipped if , , or . this is why a button "doesn't work" when an invisible overlay sits on top with interaction enabled, or why a subview drawn outside its parent's bounds can't be tapped doesn't matter — hit testing does . phase 2 — the responder chain bottom up . once the hit view is found, if it doesn't handle the event it passes it to its responder: the chain in practice actions ride this chain. this is the trick behind , , and : any responder that implements handles it. that's how the system "find" command or copy/paste reaches whatever is appropriate without hard wiring. first responder the first responder is the object that receives keyboard and text input. calling on a text field is what shows the keyboard; dismisses it. gates it. custom views can become first responder too — that's how you build a view that accepts key commands or shows an input accessory. why this matters

uiscrollview mechanics is the most important view in uikit. , , , and swiftui's are all built on it. scroll linked animations — parallax, sticky headers, the stretchy hero image, the wavy carousel — are all just functions of . the core geometry a scroll view doesn't move its content. it moves its own — which is . subviews are drawn relative to bounds, so changing the origin slides everything. this is the same origin trick from layer geometry. contentoffset drives effects the key realization: a scroll effect is , computed every frame the user scrolls — not an animation. this is category 1 from who drives time. fires on every offset change — keep it cheap no allocations, no layout . the delegate timeline of a swipe is the powerful one: you receive the velocity and a mutable pointer. rewrite that pointer to implement custom paging or snap to item: paging, insets, and the safe area snaps in viewport sized steps. for custom card widths, leave it off and snap via above. controls whether the system adds insets for the nav bar / safe area — a frequent source of "my content starts 100pt too low." try it the demo below scrolls a list where each row's scale and opacity are a pure function of its distance from center — the uikit equivalent of swiftui's . drag it. related the math of scroll driven scaling: interpolation & easing next: 04 compositional layout · hub: 00 overview

compositional layout ios 13+ replaced the pain of custom subclasses for 90% of cases. it is built from a strict, composable hierarchy — once you internalize it, almost any grid, list, carousel, or magazine layout becomes a few lines. the hierarchy: item → group → section an item is one cell. a group arranges items along one axis. a section stacks groups. a layout holds sections. fractional sizing is the superpower dimensions come in three flavors: / — relative to the container group or section . — fixed points. — a starting guess that self sizes to auto layout content great for text . because items size fractionally to their group , the same layout reflows correctly across screen sizes and orientations with no math on your part. per section layouts the real power: each section can have a completely different layout via the section provider, so a single collection view can mix a horizontal carousel, a grid, and a list: orthogonal scrolling carousels for free set the section's and the section scrolls horizontally inside a vertical collection view — the app store's shelves, with no nested scroll views: supplementary items headers, footers, and badges are pinned headers via and badges anchored to an item corner . try it adjust items per group, group count, spacing, and axis below to see how fractional sizing reflows the section. related feeding it data without crashes: 05 diffable

diffable data sources the classic / api made you responsible for keeping your model and the view in sync via . get the counts wrong and you crash: "invalid number of items." diffable data sources ios 13+ flip the model: you describe the desired state as a snapshot, and uikit computes the diff and animates it. snapshots describe state, not changes you never call / . you hand over the new truth; uikit figures out what moved, inserted, or deleted and animates each correctly. identifiers must be hashable and stable the diff is computed on identifiers , not objects. use a stable id not array index , and make sure equality is meaningful: store item identifiers in the snapshot, keep the full objects in your store, and look them up in the cell provider. this avoids re rendering everything when one field changes. if two different items hash equal, the diff misbehaves cells vanish or duplicate — the single most common diffable bug. cell registration replaces dequeue by string no more reuse identifier strings, no force casts. is the modern, value type way to fill a cell — the same configuration objects work for table and collection list cells. reloading vs reconfiguring ios 15+ updates a visible cell in place without replacing it — cheaper and flicker free for content changes where identity is unchanged. use it instead of when only the data inside a cell changed. why it matters diffable d
